symptoms.symptom_name: Bilateral pressing, band-like, non-throbbing head pain of moderate intensity

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Bilateral, pressing/tightening, non-pulsating quality of mild-moderate intensity are the core ICHD-3 features of tension-type headache.
Migraine is typically unilateral and pulsating with moderate-severe intensity.
Tension-type headache is not accompanied by nausea/vomiting; their absence is part of the ICHD-3 criteria.
Nausea/vomiting is a supporting feature of migraine; its absence weighs against migraine.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain not aggravated by routine physical activity

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lack of aggravation by routine activity is an ICHD-3 discriminator distinguishing tension-type headache from migraine.
Migraine is characteristically worsened by routine physical activity.
Tension-type headache may have EITHER mild photophobia OR mild phonophobia (not both, and no nausea); isolated mild phonophobia is compatible.
Migraine typically has both photophobia and phonophobia; isolated mild phonophobia without photophobia is less typical.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset with occupational stress, fatigue and prolonged screen time; self-reported pericranial/neck-shoulder muscle tightness and tenderness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Psychological stress and sustained poor posture/screen work are the classic precipitants; pericranial muscle tenderness supports the pericranial-tenderness subtype.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pericranial muscle tenderness on palpation of temporalis and trapezius

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objectively increased pericranial tenderness on manual palpation defines the 'with pericranial tenderness' subtype — a clinician sign, not obtainable over chat (the patient can only self-report tightness).

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal neurological examination (cranial nerves II-XII intact, 5/5 power, normal gait and reflexes) and no papilloedema on fundoscopy

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal focal neurological exam and absent papilloedema argue against a space-occupying lesion or raised intracranial pressure — reassuring but clinician-obtained, unavailable in telehealth.
Focal deficit or papilloedema would point to a secondary structural cause; both are absent.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-tender, palpable temporal arteries and blood pressure 135/85 mmHg

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: secondary headache (giant cell arteritis / hypertensive headache)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Non-tender temporal arteries argue against giant cell arteritis and a normal-range BP argues against a hypertensive cause — both are clinician-measured and sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Negative headache red-flag screen (no thunderclap onset, fever, neck stiffness, focal deficit or head injury)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: secondary headache (subarachnoid haemorrhage / meningitis / space-occupying lesion)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A comprehensive negative red-flag history is the single most important step that permits safe attribution to a benign primary headache and telehealth self-care.
Once dangerous secondary causes are screened out, the benign primary headache diagnosis is supported.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Bilateral pressing non-throbbing pain, no nausea, no activity aggravation, with a stress/postural trigger — together strongly specific for tension-type headache.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated for a classic, red-flag-negative tension-type headache; imaging is reserved for red-flag or atypical features. Over-ordering here is the error the case tests.
If headaches recur, a diary guides diagnosis of chronic/frequent tension-type headache and detects medication-overuse patterns — a patient-led, low-cost tool.
